https://community.sap.com/khhcw49343/downloadCalendar?message.id=14430488

entry.title: Bringing Domain Intelligence into SAP Joule
entry.content:
Why “domain intelligence” is the missing piece

Foundation models are impressive generalists, but enterprise questions are specific. “Which quotation gives us the best win/win with this customer?” is not answered by general world knowledge. It is answered by your master data, your pricing logic, and the relationships between them.

That is where two capabilities on SAP Business AI Platform come together. First, a Knowledge Graph captures your domain as connected, machine-readable facts with explicit semantics. Second, the A2A protocol lets a custom agent that reasons over that graph plug directly into Joule, so the intelligence shows up where users already work.

The picture below shows how the pieces fit: Joule stays the orchestrator, a pro-code agent does the specialized work, and the SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph engine holds the domain intelligence the agent draws on.

Joule orchestrates; the pro-code agent reasons; the Knowledge Graph engine holds your domain intelligence.

Step 1: Capture domain knowledge as an Ontology and Knowledge Graph

A Knowledge Graph is only as smart as the ontology behind it. The ontology is the semantic model: it names your entities, describes their attributes, and, crucially, records how they relate. That semantic layer is what turns raw content into meaning a machine can reason over.

Importantly, your source does not have to be a database. The SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph samples cover two paths. In the first, unstructured documents such as PDFs are turned into a graph: a large language model extracts the entities and the relationships between them from the text and converts them into RDF triples. In the second, tabular data is modeled as an ontology from its schema, keys and joins. Either way, the result is serialized to RDF in Turtle (.ttl), so the same downstream steps apply regardless of where the knowledge came from.

Loading it into SAP HANA Cloud is straightforward. You ingest the triples through the SPARQL_EXECUTE procedure, or stage the file in cloud storage and pull it in with IMPORT FROM RDF FILE from the Database Explorer. Once ingested, the graph is queryable with SPARQL, including from natural language, so business users can ask a question in plain words and get a grounded, relationship-aware answer.

The pipeline looks like this, from your source data on the left to a query-ready graph on the right. The labels are deliberately generic; your entities, documents and agent will be your own:

Build the graph once, then reuse it across pro-code agents and every Joule conversation.

The important idea for partners: you model the graph once. The same semantic asset can then ground many agents and many conversations, rather than being rebuilt for each use case.

Step 2: Bring your agent into Joule with A2A

Joule acts as the central orchestrator and entry point. When a user makes a request, Joule’s planning and reasoning engine decides how to fulfil it, which can include delegating the task to a custom agent. SAP supports two integration patterns, and the right one depends on how the agent was built.

For low-code agents built with Joule Studio, integration is largely automatic. Deploying the agent generates the required Joule artifacts, including a Joule Scenario and a Dialog Function, and registers the scenario in Joule’s Scenario Catalog. There is no endpoint or protocol to manage by hand.

For pro-code agents, the “Bring Your Own Agent” (BYOA) pattern uses the open A2A protocol, so you can build with any framework that speaks A2A. The flow is clean and decoupled:

  1. Expose your agent as an A2A server with an HTTP endpoint that follows the A2A specification.
  2. Create a Joule Scenario for the agent, and add a Dialog Function with an action of type agent-request.
  3. Point that Dialog Function at your agent’s A2A endpoint.

At runtime, Joule becomes the A2A client. It prepares a request using the message/send method (A2A version 0.3.0), sends the user’s utterance to your agent, waits for the response, and presents the result. Joule handles synchronous replies within a 60 second window; for longer work, the agent uses push notifications to a Joule webhook, and context and task IDs carry state across multi-turn conversations. Secure communication is established through an Identity Authentication Service (IAS) App2App trust relationship between Joule and your agent.

One practical note worth setting expectations on: the Agent Gateway is not yet generally available, so today the architecture supports unidirectional, outbound communication from Joule to the agent. The bidirectional building blocks are expected to follow.

Two building blocks today, one coherent example soon

It is worth being clear about where things stand. Today these are two separate reference assets, not one finished scenario. The Knowledge Graph samples show how to build and query a graph from your documents or tables. The pro-code agent reference, btp-joule-a2a-pro-code-agent, shows the other half: a production-grade agent that plugs into Joule over A2A.

That reference agent is written in TypeScript on CAP, uses LangGraph for its logic, reaches foundation models through the SAP Cloud SDK for AI and Generative AI Hub, and demonstrates the full A2A round trip with Joule, including human-in-the-loop and webhook-based asynchronous updates, with Terraform for the BAIP setup. A companion community walkthrough by Felix Bartler shows the minimal version of the same A2A wiring.

The target pattern joins the two halves: a pro-code agent that treats your HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph as a tool, issuing SPARQL queries to fetch precise, relationship-aware facts, then reasoning with Generative AI Hub before returning a grounded answer through Joule. The graph supplies the trustworthy facts; the model supplies the reasoning; A2A supplies the delivery.

What this unlocks for SAP partners

This is where the partner opportunity is clearest. The pattern lets you help your customers bring their own domain data, knowledge and ontology into SAP, where it becomes a governed, reusable Knowledge Graph that grounds Joule. Instead of standing up a separate AI stack, your customers leverage the SAP platform investments they already have, including SAP HANA Cloud, SAP Business AI Platform (BAIP), Generative AI Hub and Joule, while you supply the domain expertise and the agent on top. Their proprietary knowledge stays in their SAP landscape, under their governance.

The advantages compound. The Knowledge Graph is a reusable asset that encodes domain expertise once and grounds every agent that uses it, which reduces hallucination and makes answers defensible. A2A is an open standard, so you keep framework freedom and avoid lock-in while still landing natively inside the SAP experience. And because the intelligence surfaces through Joule, adoption is frictionless: customers’ users get specialized answers in the assistant they already trust.

The build path is incremental. Start by turning one high-value slice of a customer’s knowledge, a set of documents or a key data model, into an ontology and Knowledge Graph in SAP HANA Cloud. Wrap a pro-code agent around it that queries the graph and reasons with Generative AI Hub. Then expose the agent over A2A and register it as a Joule Scenario. Each step is independently useful, and together they turn generic AI into your customer’s AI.

Further resources to get you started

  • Building Intelligent Data Applications with SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graphs: https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/protected/index.html#/missiondetail/4568/4856/
  • Knowledge Graph samples (documents and tables to ontology, 7 scenarios):  github.com/IDGCOENA/saphanacloudkge
  • A2A pro-code agent reference implementation:  github.com/SAP-samples/btp-joule-a2a-pro-code-agent
  • SAP Architecture Center, Integrating AI Agents with Joule:  architecture.learning.sap.com
  • Community walkthrough, Connect Code Based Agents into Joule:  community.sap.com
  • A2A protocol specification:  a2a-protocol.org

 

Coming next - Stay tuned!

Coming next.  We are building a coherent, end-to-end example that connects a custom Knowledge Graph to a Joule agent over A2A, and we are preparing a hands-on workshop to walk partners through it.

Share your use cases and questions in the comments. Don't hesitate to reach out as well in case you want some more close support.

entry.title: How to Connect Claude Code to ABAP with Zero-Config MCP
entry.content:
Before We Start - What This Post Deliberately Skips
- What is Claude Code, and what are the available models?
- What is MCP (Model Context Protocol) and how it works conceptually?
- What is vibe coding and why AI-assisted development matters?
- What are the benefits of using AI in SAP development?
- What development platforms are available (Web, Desktop, CLI)?
If you need more details, you can find these topics in sap community portal. Read that, then come back here.

What This Post Covers - and Why It Matters?

This post walks you through connecting Claude Code to a live SAP system using the ABAP Remote Filesystem extension's native MCP server - no external Node.js setup, no manual JSON editing, just a single toggle. 

What this changes for SAP development:

  • No more hand-typed routine code. TYPE declarations, SELECT statements, ALV setup - written directly into the SAP object from a single prompt.
  • No more AI guesswork. Without a live connection, AI tools hallucinate tables and function modules that don't exist. With MCP, every suggestion is grounded in your actual system.
  • No more setup overhead. What used to require a custom Node.js project now takes one checkbox and one command - open to any developer with VS Code.
  • Clean Core by default. Claude works through the same ADT API and released objects that Eclipse uses, so generated code fits your governance model instead of fighting it.

This is the point where AI-assisted ABAP stops being a chat-window experiment and starts being a real part of your IDE.

 
Goal: I will cover the entire topic in 4 parts:
|Part 1| Installing Claude Code on Windows and setting up the VS Code extension |
|Part 2| Connecting VS Code to your live SAP system via the ABAP FS extension |
|Part 3| Enabling the native MCP server with a single toggle - no Node.js setup, no JSON editing |
|Part 4| A live demo: one plain-English prompt creates a real, running ABAP report on SAP |
If you're here, you already know why this matters. Let's get to the actual steps.
 
Pre-requisite: This guide assumes you already have an active Claude subscription (Pro, Max, Team, or Enterprise). If not, sign up at claude.ai before proceeding
 
Part 1 - The Setup: Getting Claude Code Running on Windows
Step 1.1 - Install Claude Code CLI
Open PowerShell (the built-in Windows command-line shell) and run:

 

This downloads Anthropic's official installer and executes it in one step. 'irm' fetches the script, 'iex' runs it.
> ⚠️ Common Error - Git Bash Not Found
> Claude Code needs a bash shell to run on Windows. If you see this error, install Git for Windows from [git-scm.com/downloads/win](https://git-scm.com/downloads/win). During setup, select: Git from the command line and also from 3rd-party software. Then re-run the install command above.
Step 1.2 — Verify the Installation

 

Close PowerShell and open a fresh window. Run:
You should see a version number like 'claude 1.x.x'. If PowerShell says claude is not recognized, the PATH wasn't set automatically. Fix it manually:

 

1. Start → search Environment Variables in system settings
2. Edit the environment variables for your account
3. Select Path → Edit → New → paste ( in this case path can be taken from powershell commnad prompt)
 for eg: 

 

4. Close and reopen PowerShell, then re-run claude --version.

> Run claude doctor - this command checks your PATH, authentication, Node.js version, and config in one shot. Always the first step when something isn't working.
Step 1.3 - Launch Claude Code and the VS Code Extension
Navigate to your project folder and launch Claude Code.
The first launch opens your browser to authenticate with your Anthropic account (Pro, Max, Team, or Enterprise all work). 
For everyday ABAP work, the VS Code extension is where you'll spend most of your time.
Download and Install VS code from code.visualstudio.com for windows if you don't already have it. Once it is installed.Open VS Code, press 'Ctrl+Shift+X', search for Claude Code (published by Anthropic), and install it. Once installed, the Claude icon appears in the Activity Bar and connects automatically to your account.

 

The extension gives you a persistent chat panel inside VS Code - ask Claude about the file you have open, highlight any ABAP block and ask it to explain or refactor, and switch between ABAP and CAP projects in the same session. No terminal switching required.
> Note on Software Center installs: If your organization restricts installations, Claude Code for VS Code is typically available via your company Software Center / Company Portal.
 
Part 2 - Connecting VS Code to Your SAP System via ABAP FS
This is where the magic begins. The ABAP Remote Filesystem (ABAP FS)extension - built by Marcello Urbani - connects VS Code to your SAP system using the same ADT REST API that Eclipse uses. Once connected, your ABAP objects (programs, classes, CDS views, behavior definitions) appear as files in VS Code. Claude Code can then read, write, and review them like any local code file.
Pre-requisites Before You Connect
You'll need these four things from your SAP system before starting:
| Hostname or IP | The address of your SAP server (hostname preferred - IP causes SSL issues explained below) |
| ADT Service active | Go to transaction SICF → '/sap/bc/adt' must be activated |
| Client number| e.g. '100' - visible in the SAP logon screen |
|Username & Password| Your regular SAP development user |
| Port | HTTPS: '44300' (typical) | HTTP: '8000' for instance 00, '8001' for instance 01 |
 
Step 2.1 - Install and Configure ABAP FS
1. In VS Code Extensions ('Ctrl+Shift+X'), search ABAP remote filesystem by Marcello Urbani → Install.
2. Press 'Ctrl+Shift+P' → type ABAP FS: Connection Manager → open it.
3. Add a new connection and fill in the fields:
   - Name: anything you like (e.g. DEV or SANDBOX)
   - Server URL:'https://<hostname>:44300'
   - ⚠️ Do NOT add '/sap/bc/adt' to the URL - the extension adds this automatically. If you include it, the path doubles and the connection fails.
   - Client, Username, Language: 'EN'
4. Save.
5. Press 'Ctrl+Shift+P' → ABAP FS: Connect to an SAP system→ select your connection → enter password.
 

 

> ⚠️ SSL Certificate Error (very common on internal systems)
> If you see IP does not match certificate's altnames - this is because your SAP system's SSL certificate was issued with a hostname, not the IP. The fix: Get the hostname (e.g. 'https://sapdev.company.com:44300') and use that instead of the IP. 
> ⚠️ADT Service Not Found
> If you see Resource /sap/bc/adt does not exist - the ADT service isn't activated. Run tcode SICF → navigate to default_host → 'sap/bc/adt' → right-click → Activate Service.
 
Step 2.2 - Navigating ABAP Objects
ABAP FS gives you two ways to find objects. Object Search lets you find anything by name instantly. If you prefer a tree view like Eclipse ADT, enable SAP GUI Integration in your connection settings - this gives you a full package tree (Dictionary, Source Code Library, Transactions) and even embeds SAP GUI for HTML directly inside VS Code, so you can run transactions without leaving the editor.
1. Click OBJECT SEARCH in the left sidebar
2. Type the object or package name (e.g. 'ZCL_MY_CLASS' or 'Z_MY_PACKAGE')
3. Double-click any result to open it as a text file in VS Code
4. To edit: right-click  → edit → save (changes push back to SAP automatically)
Once an object is open in VS Code, open the integrated terminal (Ctrl+) and run claude. Some prompts that immediately pay off:

 

Part 3 -The Game Changer: Enabling the Native MCP Server

 

This is the step that changes everything - and the reason this post exists.
Why ABAP FS Alone Isn't Enough?
With only ABAP FS connected, Claude can see only the one file you currently have open. Ask it to create a new class and it might invent table names, function modules, or BAdI definitions that don't exist on your system. It's helpful, but it's guessing.
          ABAP FS Only                                         ABAP FS + MCP
Claude sees what we search Manually Claude autonomously searches any object on the system
Hallucinates objects it hasn't seen Grounded in live SAP system data
You copy-paste code manually Claude reads, writes, activates autonomously
No transport awareness Creates & assigns transports via ADT

 

The MCP (Model Context Protocol) server gives Claude Code a live connection to your SAP system's ADT API - the same backend Eclipse uses. Claude stops guessing and starts knowing.
Step 3.1 - Enable the MCP Server (One Toggle)
This is where the zero-config part comes in. You don't need to install anything extra, clone a Node.js repo, or edit JSON files manually.
Press 'Ctrl+Shift+P' → search ABAP FS MCP → select it → enable Autostart.
That's it. The ABAP FS extension spins up a local MCP server on your machine (default port 4847). You'll see a VS Code notification confirming it's running:
> ABAP MCP Server running on port 4847. External AI clients can connect to http://localhost:4847/mcp

Step 3.2 - Register the MCP Server with Claude Code
Now tell Claude Code where to find the ABAP tools. Run this in the VS Code integrated terminal:
This registers the local MCP server with Claude Code. The configuration is saved to your '.claude.json' file and persists across sessions - you only need to do this once.
Step 3.3 - Verify the Connection

 

open the terminal and run this command once the MCP server is added 
You should see:
Claude Code will confirm when the server is registered:
> abap-remote-fs has been added with HTTP transport at http://localhost:4847/mcp"*
The Complete Architecture
Claude Code can now autonomously:
- Search and read any ABAP object on the live SAP system
- write → syntax check → activate → unlock objects
- Create transport requests and assign objects
- Run ATC checks and read short dumps - all from a plain-English prompt
> Golden - In Auto mode, Claude will write, syntax check, and activate without pausing - that's the power of the agentic workflow. If you want to review before anything is activated, switch to Plan mode  shift+tab to cycle modes)  Claude will show you the full plan and wait for your approval. For production-adjacent systems, always run in Plan or Step mode.
Part 4 - Live Demo: One Prompt, One Real ABAP Program on SAP
Disclaimer: This is my first time setting up all this and explore, so i just started with a very simple scenario. Going forward will create RAP objects and other complex artifacts and see the efficiency.
Let me show you what all of this enables. Here's the exact prompt I used:
> Create a new ABAP executable program ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01 in my ZVS_DEMO package. Fetch data from MARA for a user-specified Material Number (select-option) and display Material Number, Type, and Created By in an ALV grid. Write in ABAP 7.5 syntax.
One prompt. No templates. No copy-paste. Here's what Claude did.
Act 1 - Claude Plans Before It Codes
Claude didn't immediately start writing code. It:
1. Confirmed the target package ('ZVS_DEMO') exists on the system
2. Detected that the program creation triggered a **transport request dialog** — and recognised it was the likely source of a cancellation
3. Called the SAP ADT API via Bash to **list existing open transports**
4. Selected the correct transport and retried the creation
There was a little bit delay on my end to confirm the transport. and it gave me this message.
> Package confirmed. The cancellation is likely a transport request dialog. Let me check for existing transports, then retry.
This is the part that impresses ABAP developers most. Claude handled a real SAP workflow quirk .
Act 2 - The Program Appears on SAP
Claude then created the program on the live system. You can watch its autonomous todo list mid-execution:
- Create ABAP program ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01 in ZVS_DEMO package via MCP
- Get workspace URI for the new program
- Write ABAP source code into the program
- Activate the program and check diagnostics
'ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01' appeared in the VS Code Explorer sidebar - a real, registered ABAP program on the SAP system, not a local file.
You can see initially there was no program created in my package 
After few seconds, the program is created 

 

Act 3 - Full ABAP 7.5 Source Code Written
With the object created, Claude fetched the workspace URI and wrote the complete source code directly into the SAP program. What it generated:
No placeholder code. Real, working ABAP in the correct syntax release, referencing actual MARA field types from the live system.
Act 4 - Running the Program
After activation, there are two ways to run it:
Option A - Classic SAP GUI:Log in to SAP GUI →run the program → Execute
Option B - Embedded SAP Web GUI(what I used): VS Code can open the SAP Web GUI directly in a browser tab, embedded inside the IDE. No separate login or window switching required.
Enter selection criteria:
The selection screen appeared with the Matnr  field exactly as specified. I entered a range of 11to 21. The value help (F4) shows the available materials in the system
Material Number, Material Type, and Created By - exactly what was asked for. Live data from MARA. Report running.
 
Summary:
|Claude understands SAP context| It identified the transport dialog, queried existing transports via ADT, and handled a real SAP workflow quirk autonomously 
| It created a real SAP object| 'ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01' exists on the live system, created via ADT REST API through MCP - not a file on disk 
| Production-quality ABAP 7.5 | TYPE declarations referencing actual MARA field types, correct naming conventions, modern SQL syntax 
| Minutes, not hours| The developer's job shifts to reviewing - not writing skeletons, hunting transport dialogs, or looking up field types 
| Fully iterative| Once created, you can ask Claude to add authority checks, error handling, ALV field catalogues, or additional filters - each iteration in seconds 
| You stay in control | Claude's todo list is transparent - you see every step it plans. You review the code. You decide what activates 
 
Where to Go from Here?
Here are five things to try once you have the setup working:
1. Understand inherited code - Explain the purpose and flow of report Z_*_REPORT. Include tables accessed, authority checks, deprecated patterns like SELECT .
2. Generate a CDS view - Create a CDS view hierarchy for Sales Order analysis: interface view joining VBAK+VBAP, projection view with Fiori Elements. UI annotations for a List Report.
3. Build a Travel Request POC -  RAP Business Object with CRUD, approve/reject action, draft handling, OData V4 binding, Fiori Elements UI.
4. Write ABAP Unit tests - Generate a unit test class 
5. Code review and modernization - Review this class for Clean ABAP violations, missing authority checks, SELECT * usage, and SELECT inside LOOP. Propose S/4-equivalent replacements.
 
The bottom line: The integration that used to require manual Node.js setup, JSON editing, and external package management now takes a single Ctrl+Shift+P toggle and one terminal command.
 
The ABAP Remote Filesystem team has done the hard work. Claude Code does the coding. You do the review.
Give it a try - and let me know in the comments what use case you tackle first.
 
A personal note  : This is my first time diving deeper into vibe coding and MCP concepts. I come from a classic ABAP background, and everything covered here -  from the VS Code setup to the MCP server to the live demo - I explored and built from scratch over the past few weeks. The scenario is intentionally simple. The goal was to get the fundamentals right, document them honestly, and share what works. There is a lot more ground to cover - RAP generation at scale, Clean Core extensibility patterns, ATC pre-checks via Claude Code, and deeper agentic workflows. I'll be going further in the posts ahead. If you're also just getting started with this, I hope this gives you a solid, working foundation to build on. And if you're already further ahead - I'd love to hear what you've learnt in the comments.
 
Note: All screenshots in this post are taken from a live S/4HANA development system. Code generated by Claude Code should always be reviewed and tested in a sandbox environment before activation in any productive system.

entry.title: AI-Assisted Journal Upload
entry.content:
I am Betty Guedez, Product & Engineering Solution Architect at SAP Regional Implementation Group RIG. I am coming back to this community with an SAP strategic topic: Business AI.

 

            More specifically, Autonomous Finance. AI is the cornerstone of SAP Autonomous Enterprise offering, that revolutionize the way we support our customers businesses: Innovations that develop state-of the-art tools and agents to automate tasks, where humans can be released of cumbersome tasks to focus on business intelligence and financial engineering to maximize benefits with real-time value.

           

            As part of the SAP Autonomous Enterprise, SAP Autonomous Finance is built out of Joule Assistants that orchestrate all the Finance agents that support this business process. AI-assisted Journal Upload is part of Financial Closing Assistant, that drives the full close lifecycle across posting, accruals, journal validation, reconciliations, and so on. The major goal of Financial Closing Assistant is to reducing closing time and streamlines the reporting associated to closing.

 

 

Figure 1: SAP Finance Assistants-Financial Closing Assistant

 

            For years, SAP has developed solutions that enable customers to perform specific business tasks which, while executed outside the ERP system and often requiring significant time and effort, provide critical input for gaining a complete view of the business. Tasks that, if not performed, can disrupt business operations and compromise data visibility. Uploading journal entries is one of them. It is either a periodic or frequent activity or both, in every landscape: satellite systems, legacies, integrations that support the operation in a way or another, that feedback the ERP with transactions that must be posted to keep the accounting books updated.

 

            Many of us have used the different ways to upload journal entries to the ERP: The traditional file (we used to call it "flat") with a fixed structure of columns with specific format. Another is the journal entry template, for postings based on your needs, where fields and values can be selected to produce a tailored-specific template for postings, taking out non-relevant fields and focusing only on the desired ones. This template can also be shared and with pre-defined level of use (group, company, user). Please look at Fiori app Manage Journal Entry Templates (F3803)documentation to know of this, in case you are interested. Both have been extremely useful tools, widely used by customers and partners.

            Month-end journal entries upload is more needed than ever. Increasingly complex system landscapes, new integration points, constantly evolving business models, and the ever-changing business environment demonstrate that, if this process can be executed autonomously with minimal supervision, the resulting efficiency gains and cost savings can make a significant contribution to the organization's value creation efforts. 

What is AI-Assisted Journal Upload?

 

            As period-end journal entries are necessary for period closing, accounting teams are spending considerable time in curating data as per the policies and reporting needs. Through new SAP Fiori apps with Generative AI capabilities embedded, AI-assisted Journal Upload in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition accelerates the creation and the upload of period-end manual journal entries.

            AI-assisted Journal Upload is a Financial AI feature to speed-up the creation and the upload of period-end manual journal entries.

            With AI-assisted Journal Upload, accountants can:

  • Assign a guidance document (written in natural language) for posting to the journal upload case
  • Create journal upload cases
  • Generate journal posting proposals according to the assigned guidance
  • Validate the posting proposals before posting and
  • Post the posting proposal as journals

 

What are typical business cases for AI-Assisted Journal Upload?

            Business scenarios best suited for AI-assisted Journal Upload include recurring general ledger journal entries with a high volume of records and similar information requirements, making them ideal candidates for this autonomous feature. Other excellent use cases include large volumes of prepaid expenses, lease or rental in advance-payments, and customer prepayments, all of which can be efficiently processed using AI-assisted Journal Upload.

Is AI-assisted Journal Upload intended for large companies only?

 

            Not at all. Regardless of their size, Companies often pay for services they will receive in the future. Small and large businesses work with prepaid expenses frequently. It is a way to secure better terms, benefit from early-payment discounts, and take advantage of periods of stronger cash flow while ensuring futre obligations are covered.

 

            When the volume of prepaid operations is high, as they happen in advance, a proper tracking is crucial for financial control and compliance, to record them when is right to do it (in the posting period when each service is received). Plus, a posting template easy to use and populate to post to the right accounting objects, is invaluable for saving time and reducing the risk of errors during the financial closing. The easiest, the better. These are some of the advantages of using AI-assisted Journal Upload.

 

            In the graphic below, we find some interesting figures about uploading journal entries for a business (complexity varies in each case). It can be found on SAP Discovery Center - S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition - AI-Assisted Journal Upload:

 

 

Figure 2: AI-assisted Journal Upload - Estimated benefits

            Technically speaking, AI-Assisted Journal Upload is an embedded Intelligent Scenario delivered via SAP's Intelligent Scenario Lifecycle Management (ISLM) framework, using scenario FIN_INTLGNT_JRNLUPLD. It is natively embedded within SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, with the underlying AI service provisioned through SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) as a backend infrastructure integration — not a side-by-side deployment.

What are the specifications of AI-Assisted Journal Upload?

  • Identifier: J247
  • AI Type: AI Feature
  • Support Component: FI-GL-AI-IJU
  • Minimun Required System Version: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2023 FPS03
  • Required Roles & Authorizations:
    • BTP: Global Account Administrator role, assigned via Security > Role Collections in the BTP Cockpit
    • S/4HANA: ISLM Administrator role to manage intelligent scenario activation
  • BTP Subaccount & Entitlement Requirements:
    • A valid BTP subaccount must exist and be accessible
    • The AI service entitlement must be available and assigned to the relevant subaccount prior to service instance creation

 

What do we need to start using AI-assisted Journal Upload?

 

 

            3 easy steps to determine AI-assisted Journal Upload readiness:

  • Check 1 - To verify the Scenario Status in Intelligent Scenario Management (F4470)  Fiori app, you need to confirm that FIN_INTLGNT_JRNLUPLD shows a Published status with no errors in the ISLM app
 
 

 

 

Figure 3: Intelligent Scenario Management

 
 

 

Figure 4: Manage Journal Posting Policies
Check 2 - Confirm that Fiori App Manage Journal Posting Policies (F8539) is available in Fiori Launchpad (FLP). Check role authorizations and catalogs if it is not visible

 

 
 

 

Figure 5: Manage Journal Upload Cases
Check 3 - Confirm that Fiori App Manage Journal Upload Cases (F8538) is available in Fiori Launchpad (FLP). Same considerations as F8539 here.

 

            Also, it is always good to make sure that content in SAP Note 3573144 AI-assisted Journal Upload in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private has been implemented in the system.

            Let me share the link to my blog post AI-assisted Journal Upload - How to use it 

            I hope you find this insightful. In my next blog, I will go step by step on how to use AI-assisted Journal Upload.

Thank you,

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA RIG team

entry.title: AI-Assisted Journal Upload - How to use it
entry.content:
I am Betty Guedez, Product & Engineering Solution Architect at SAP Regional Implementation Group RIG. I am coming back with the second part of my blog post AI-assisted Journal Upload . This time, how to use it.

 

            Remember that AI-Assisted Journal Upload is an AI feature, part of the SAP Autonomous Finance offering.

 

            We also know that SAP Autonomous Finance is one of the Joule Assistants in SAP Autonomous Enterprise platform, and it is there to that orchestrate all the Finance agents that support this business process. AI-assisted Journal Upload is part of Financial Closing Assistant, that drives the full close lifecycle across posting, accruals, journal validation, reconciliations, and so on.

 

           

Start using AI-assisted Journal Upload

 

            AI-assisted Journal Upload requires a couple of IMG-customizing-related activities in the backend, related to the category and subcategory of the journal entries you will post. Following the path Financial Accounting => Financial Accounting Global Settings => Document => Intelligent Journal Uploads, you will see the two activities, Define Journal Upload Case Categories and Define Journal Upload Case Subcategories. Together, they both serve to give a classification to the expenses that better explain your postings.

Define Journal Upload Case Categories:

Figure 1: Define Journal Upload Case Categories

 Define Journal Upload Case Subcategories:

Figure 2: Define Journal Upload Case Subcategories


            Next, to download and adapt the policy template, we access the Fiori App Manage Journal Posting Policies (F8539). With this app, we set up all the relevant fields and the natural language instructions to populate the fields on the template.

 
 

 

Figure 3: Manage Journal Posting Policies - Initial Screen


            Click on Download Template, select the format (Spreadsheet, PDF) and OK. (I feel more comfortable with the excel file, so I chose the first one).

 
 

 

Figure 4: Manage Journal Posting Policies - Template Downloaded

            Follow the guidelines provided and update the Input instructions that the feature  is going to use to handle the data in the input file. It is important to bear in mind:

  • For now, Cardinality - Optional/Mandatory (O/M)- field is not required. It will be supported in future releases, so you may leave it blank.
  • The file name format must be "Name", followed by a "_", followed by the "Language Code".ext (xslx, pdf). Supported language codes are EN (English), DE (German), ES (Spanish), FR (French), PT (Portuguese), JA (Japanese), ZH (Simplified Chinese). Example: "Policy Document 2026_EN.xlsx".
  • Make sure that the fields referenced in the Input Instruction of a field in the policy document, are columns in the input data file, for example:
 
 

 

Figure 5: Manage Journal Posting Policies - Template - Input Instruction examples

            Based on the instruction for field WRBTR, there should be a column labeled "Amount" with values in the input file.


            And now, let's work with the policy in the system:

Figure 6: Manage Journal Posting Policies - Policy Creation

Case category and Subcategory: According to customizing mentioned above

Description: Short Text about the policy purpose (max 50 characters)

Valid to: Defaulted to 12/31/9999

Valid from: Starting validity date of the policy. If there is an existing policy of the same Category and Subcategory, with Valid from date prior or equal to this, the system will raise an error, and the policy cannot be saved. The policy must start after the starting date of any previously created policy of this service.

 
 

 

Figure 7: Manage Journal Posting Policies - Policy Activation

 

 

            Once created, the policy needs to be Activated.

            Now, it is the time to upload the input data to post the journal entries.

            We designed a simplified excel file that contains the data of the charges received for shuttle services for 3 employees, to create the postings for the expenses charged for this service:

Figure 8: Manage Journal Upload Cases: Sample input data


            To do that, open the Fiori app Manage Journal Upload Cases (F8538) and hit Create:

Figure 9: Manage Journal Upload Cases: Updating the Posting Proposal

            Once created, a Proposal must be generated:

Figure 10: Manage Journal Upload Cases: Generating the Posting Proposal

            Proposal Validation:

Figure 11: Manage Journal Upload Cases: Validating the Posting Proposal

            And proposal finally to be posted:

Figure 12: Manage Upload Cases: Posting the Proposal

           

            Let's display the postings in the Fiori app Manage Journal Entries (F0717A):

 

Figure 13: Manage Journal Entries: Displaying the Postings

 

            The posted document SA 1000000006 shows that rides of the three employees using the shuttle bus service in the second week of June 2026 were posted to the right posting period (06/2026), crediting the prepaid expense (which is currently a right, in consequence) for $264,00 USD.

            AI-assisted Journal Upload is a key component of the SAP Autonomous Enterprise offering and provides an easy, straightforward process to generate postings in S/4HANA from external sources, as part of the month-end closing, releasing the business experts from spending lots of time and effort fulfilling templates that can be well known, but still somewhat error-prone.

            Before closing, let me share two more useful resources with you.

SAP AI Services in Discovery Center

AI-assisted Journal Upload in help.sap.com

            I hope you find this insightful.

Thank you,

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA RIG team

entry.title: Setting Up SAP Joule on S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: A Technical Guide
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Joule is SAP’s generative AI copilot designed to enhance user productivity across SAP applications. In SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition, Joule empowers users to discover applications, access contextual business insights, and navigate business processes conversationally directly within the SAP Fiori experience.

This document provides a comprehensive, end-to-end technical guide for setting up SAP Joule with SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition. It is written from an administrator and platform engineering perspective, explaining not only how to configure each component, but also why it is required and how the overall integration functions.

Architecture Overview

 

At a high level, Joule runs on SAP BTP and is embedded into the SAP user experience using SAP Build Work Zone. Authentication and authorization are handled centrally through SAP Cloud Identity Services.

Prerequisites and Readiness Checklist

Before proceeding with the setup, ensure the following prerequisites are in place:

Authorizations

  • SAP BTP Global Account Administrator
  • SAP BTP Subaccount Administrator
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS & IPS) Administrator
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Administrator

Entitlements

  • SAP Joule (Digital Assistant Service – das-application)
  • SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition

Landscape Requirements

  • Supported for both 2-System Landscape (2SL) and 3-System Landscape (3SL)
  • SAP Cloud Connector is not required for Public Cloud systems

Technical Preconditions

  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud tenant registered in BTP System Landscape
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant available and accessible
  • Browser allows iFrames and third-party cookies

Preparation

SAP BTP Subaccount Strategy

A dedicated SAP BTP subaccount is typically used to host Joule and SAP Build Work Zone. From a design perspective, regional alignment with SAP Cloud Identity Services is recommended to minimize authentication latency and reduce cross-region dependencies.

The subaccount acts as the orchestration layer where:

  • Joule services are provisioned
  • Work Zone acts as the user entry point
  • Trust and identity integration converge

Identity Trust and Domain Alignment
Single Sign-On between SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP BTP, and SAP Build Work Zone is achieved through trust establishment with SAP Cloud Identity Services.

A critical design consideration here is domain alignment:

  • The IAS domain selected must align with the domain model of the SAP S/4HANA Cloud tenant
  • Mismatched domains are a frequent root cause of repeated authentication prompts and intermittent Joule access issues

Trusted domain configuration on both BTP and IAS is essential for enabling secure cross-origin communication and embedded user experiences.

Identity Attribute Readiness
Joule and SAP Build Work Zone rely on a consistent identity model provided by IAS. Certain core user attributes must be available and consumable by downstream applications.

From an operational perspective:

  • Attribute completeness matters more than attribute volume
  • Missing verification-related attributes can result in silent authentication failures that are difficult to trace at runtime

Ensuring identity consistency early on significantly reduces troubleshooting effort later.

Joule Enablement Model
Guided Onboarding via Booster

SAP provides a Joule Booster that automates the majority of the technical enablement steps. This guided onboarding mechanism establishes the baseline configuration required for Joule to operate within the BTP subaccount.

Conceptually, the booster:

  • Validates entitlements and platform readiness
  • Establishes system formations
  • Creates the necessary connectivity artifacts
  • Activates SAP-delivered communication scenarios

 

Architecturally, this step should be treated as a managed onboarding process rather than a manual configuration exercise.

SAP Build Work Zone Integration

SAP Build Work Zone serves as the experience layer through which Joule is surfaced to end users. Key design elements include:

  • Subscription to the Work Zone service
  • Secure consumption of S/4HANA Cloud content
  • Centralized role-based navigation and access control.

Work Zone consumes S/4HANA Cloud content using SAP-delivered exposure mechanisms, ensuring that only authorized applications, catalogs, and groups are visible.

Content Exposure Model
Content exposure from SAP S/4HANA Cloud into SAP Build Work Zone is governed by standard communication scenarios delivered by SAP. These scenarios enable controlled sharing of Fiori content without custom integration.

From a best-practice perspective:

  • Content exposure should be validated, not modified
  • Role and catalog assignments remain the authoritative control mechanism

This approach preserves clean-core principles while enabling rich user experiences.

Identity Provisioning Architecture
Identity Provisioning Services (IPS) are used to synchronize users and role assignments into SAP Build Work Zone.

Rather than being treated as a one-time activity, identity provisioning should be viewed as:

  • A continuous synchronization mechanism
  • A dependency for consistent authorization behavior
  • A key operational component for Joule visibility

Operational validation of IPS jobs and logs is a critical checkpoint during the initial enablement.

Validation and Operational Readiness
Once the platform setup is complete, validation should focus on the end-to-end user experience rather than on individual configuration items.

Typical validation checkpoints include:

  • Successful single sign-on from SAP S/4HANA Cloud
  • Visibility of SAP Build Work Zone content
  • Availability of the Joule interaction entry point
  • Execution of basic conversational queries

A successful validation confirms that Joule is correctly embedded and operational.

 

Common Operational Observations
Based on real-world implementations, the following patterns are frequently observed:

  • Repeated login prompts usually indicate trust or domain misalignment
  • Missing Joule entry points are commonly linked to role assignment or identity synchronization gaps
  • Empty or incomplete content typically points to content exposure or IPS issues

Systematically addressing these areas resolves the majority of post-enablement issues.
